sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: A heuristic function h(n) estimates the cost from node n to the nearest goal state. It's an educated guess based on problem-specific knowledge — for example, straight-line distance is a common heuristic for road-navigation problems.

sections.heading: Admissible Heuristic
sections.body: A heuristic is admissible if it never overestimates the true cost to reach the goal — it's always optimistic or exactly correct, never pessimistic. Admissibility is what guarantees A* search (covered later) finds the optimal solution.

sections.heading: Consistent Heuristic
sections.body: A heuristic is consistent (or "monotonic") if, for every node n and successor n' generated by an action with cost c, h(n) never exceeds c + h(n'). Every consistent heuristic is also admissible, though the reverse isn't guaranteed.

sections.heading: Designing Heuristics
sections.body: Good heuristics are typically derived by relaxing the original problem — solving a simplified version of it exactly, then using that simplified solution's cost as an estimate for the real problem. Relaxed-problem heuristics are naturally admissible, because removing constraints can only make the simplified problem cheaper, never more expensive, to solve.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.body: Common heuristic examples include straight-line (Euclidean) distance for route-finding, Manhattan distance for grid-based movement problems, and the number of misplaced tiles (or total tile-distance) for the classic sliding-puzzle problem.

quiz.question: A heuristic function h(n) estimates:
options: The cost already spent reaching n
The cost from n to the nearest goal
The number of children n has
The total size of the state space

quiz.question: What does it mean for a heuristic to be admissible?
options: It always overestimates the true cost
It never overestimates the true cost to the goal
It only works on graphs, not trees

quiz.question: A common way to design an admissible heuristic is to:
options: Solve a relaxed (simplified) version of the problem and use its cost as the estimate
